speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 16, 1996. The survivor is George Eisenberg. The interviewer is Ellen Adler. We're in Fresh Meadows, New York, United States of America. And the language being used is English. My name is Ellen Adler. Today I'm interviewing Mr. George Eisenberg, in Fresh Meadows, New York, United States of America. And it is October 16th, 1996. May I please have your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my name is George Eisenberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-I-S-E-N-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was Jona Eisenberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Jona please?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-O-N-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you now?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sasow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell Sasow, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. S-A-S-O-W. Poland, you want me to spell Poland too?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's fine, thank you. [LAUGHS] What can you tell me about your first recollections of your childhood in Sasow? Tell me about your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents, they were-- we lived in a-- well, there was-- where we lived was a small town. And my-- my mother was a homemaker. My father was like what it's called, a merchant. He used to go out from different places and deal with all kind of porters and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Nuchim. N-U-C-H-I-M. And my mother's name was Risa. It was-- the way we spell it in Polish was R-A-S-I-A-- Risa, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had two brothers, no sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they older or younger than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were younger. I was the oldest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me their names and when they were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The-- my-- this-- my-- my next one after me, was his name was Hershel. And the youngest one was Aaron. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a home did you live in? Was it a house or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we had like, a small house. It belonged to my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there a large Jewish community in Sasow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Sasow was a small town to begin with. But there was, I would say, about 1,500 Jewish people before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea what percentage that would be of the whole community?

speaker: interviewee
text: What it became?

speaker: interviewer
text: No--

speaker: interviewee
text: The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that-- were half the people there not Jewish? Or what percentage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what percentage? I would say, maybe about at least 30% were Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Tell me about your education. What kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when the-- when the war broke out I was only, what? 14 years old. And I had-- I went to elementary school and I think I went one of two classes in high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me a little bit about how you celebrated your religion at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- our family was a religious family. My grandparents lived with us. And my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My grandfather's name-- I mean, this is from the mother side-- was-- his name was Zelig. And my grandma's name was [? Bella. ?] So [? Bella, ?] they used to call her. And they were very religious people. We were very observant Jews. We used to observe every holiday. We used to observe-- Saturday Shabbos. And it was a-- a very-- a very religious home, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you bar mitzvahed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- the bar mitzvah, I was bar mitzvah. But not like they made a bar mitzvah for my grandson. Well, I remember in Sasow, when they made a bar mitzvah, they used to call you up to the Torah. First of all, you had to know how to daven. You had to know about Judaism. You had to know about chametz, and so on. They just call you up to the Torah. My grandma baked some cookies. And my father was bringing a bottle of vodka. And that Shabbos we went to shul and we made a kiddush. And my father would say, after I made the haftarah, after I said-- he would say [HEBREW]. Which means, in English, thanks, God. I'm [INAUDIBLE] since. That was the bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Do you remember, whether any of your friends or your parents' friends were non-Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a small town. We had friends. But to be honest with you, this town there in Poland-- I mean, actually it belong to the Ukraine. And it was always antisemitic people. And they did not associate too much with the Jews. And they always used to make fun of the Jews. I mean, but we lived OK. We went along all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your father ever explaining to you why the others treated you badly or made fun of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, it never came up, that question. I mean, before the war it was OK. We used to go to school together with non-Jewish kids. And we got along OK. Just when the war started, that was the bad part.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to begin with, the-- when the World War II started out in 1939, the Russians made a deal with the Germans, that they're going to occupy the certain part of Poland. And the Germans were going to occupy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in our town, we belonged to the Russian side. So the Russians came in, in 1939. And life was pretty good. And then the-- the Russians were with us till 1941. Did you continue going to school during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Russian, yeah. But it wasn't like it used to be, you know? It was break up in classes, you know? And there was not too much going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father continue working?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, yeah. He had no choice. He had to go out and to earn a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did things change when--

speaker: interviewee
text: To begin with, the Germans came in to our town, I think, in July or September-- or in August, they came in. And the first week they came in, it was OK. Then the next time, the next week, what they did, it's-- life started to be horrible. They-- I remember once when they came in. And once it was on a Friday afternoon. We had no-- our streets were not paved. No cement and no concrete or tar. It was like mud.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what they did, they gathered-- they took all the Jews, most of them, whoever they could find. And they gathered them. They took them out in-- to the place in the city, where-- on the roads, on the streets.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they told them to clean the mud with their hands. No shovels, nothing. After we clean the mud, everybody was muddy. So we went home. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you were involved in this also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was involved, yes. Because I didn't want my father to go. So I went. And then one time, what they did-- and this was once, and a couple of weeks later-- they came up with another idea. They got it, they took all the people who had the beards. You know, because mostly the holy people, the pious people, they took them. And they took them out in the center of the town. And they told them to put their tallits on, which is the prayer shawls. And they told them to-- and then they gave them rifles. And then they told the German soldiers to go up there, against, in the opposite way, and to raise their hands, to make believe that the Jews are killing Germans. The next day, it was all over the papers. They're showing pictures that the Jews are killing the advanced German Army, and that the Germans have to take revenge on this. On a Saturday morning, while the Jews were in the synagogue, they came in. And they made a pogrom. They took out, they killed-- they took on that time, they killed between 20 and 30 people. They just clobbered them to death. Not even shot-- shoot. Then they took all the women and all the kids, I was one of them. And they put all these-- not even dead bodies. And they put them on the wagon. And we were the horses who were pulling the wagons, bring them over to the cemetery for burial. They brought all these people to the cemetery. And they told us to dig a big ditch without any show, without anything. And then they took all these corpses, all these people, some with-- their screams are still here. And they told us to cover this there. And they put all these in one mass grave. And then they said they're going to kill us too. We were maybe 20, 30 people-- I don't remember-- women and children. In the meantime, it was came to darkness, in the evening. One German officer came on a horse with his whip. And he start screaming. He says, Jews run home. Run-- run away from here. And this is how that day end. And this--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the soldiers did not have orders to shoot after the retreating bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, after-- after the-- after this officer came along, they told us to run back home. And they didn't shoot us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you known any of the people that you had to bury?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I knew them. I don't remember their name, but I knew them all, sure. I mean, it was a small town. It was one-- one know the other one. By the way, you're talking about these pogroms. I got to mention one story also. Because you were asking about the neighbors, or about the kids [INAUDIBLE]. You know, in the Jewish religion, you're not allowed to do any cooking or to light any candles after Shabbos, during the Shabbos. So we used to have one guy who used to come around in the whole town. And he was what they call him, the Shabbos goy He was the one who would go in and put on the fires. Because we had no electricity and no water. And he used to be the one who used to come in on Saturday morning and put on the fire for the stove and for the oven. And so on. And everybody, Monday, they used to pay him a few pennies, a few zloties, whatever, some food and everything. He was helping. This was going on all the time. When the Germans came in, he was the one who was that got-- he decided who shall live who shall die. When the Germans lined up all these people the first time, and they took all the men and they lined them up in the center of town, and they asked-- his name was a-- a Ukrainian name. His name was Yaska Volk. And he was the [INAUDIBLE]. He was the one, like he would tell you, this one goes on this side. And the other one goes on this side. And this one goes on this side, and this one goes on this side. He was the selector. And he's the one-- this was the thank you, what he gave to the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to go back on one thing before we go on with your story. Can you tell me a little about the synagogue that you attended, and the rabbi? who was the head of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, the-- we had three synagogues. And the rabbis, you know, to tell you who the rabbi was-- you know, that's-- we had a few of us. But I don't remember the-- Rabbi [? Tauv ?] was one of them, I remember. And the synagogues, you know, the synagogues, they were-- the-- the synagogues-- let me put it to you this way. The synagogues in our town was not a synagogue like in here, where it's a business, synagogue. And the synagogue in the-- they didn't ask you if you have money or you don't have money. Everybody who wanted to come here was more than welcome. We did have-- we had no professional Cantor. We had a rabbi which the-- the city, the town supported him. I mean, people who were well-to-do, we used to support the rabbi. And we had Jewish teachers for teaching Hebrew. I mean, there to bar mitzvah. And it was taken care of. And the-- the synagogue, every Saturday it was full. Ever holiday it was full. People used to come. Women used to come. And children used to come. Everybody was OK. And it was not like you have to belong to this center or you belong to the other center. It was normal thing. It was nothing which was out ordinary, which [INAUDIBLE]. And they-- the town, the people, the Jewish people, they supported their rabbi. And they supported this synagogue as much as they could. And it was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have contact with a Rabbi Leib?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Rabbi Moshe Leib, he was-- you know, he was-- he was the one-- he was very-- the way the stories go, that he was very like, a pious. He was a very holy man. And he used to predict what things are going to be. But he was-- that was going back in the years, 1800 and 1900s. So I wasn't even born at that time. So I had no contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time your grandparents were still living with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents, that's one thing-- my grandparents were living with my parents. We were three children, me and my father and my mother. And then my grandmother and my grandfather. And we all lived-- we didn't have like, a six-bedroom apartment and three bathrooms. It was small. It was-- we only had, what, about two, three rooms altogether. And we had no running water, no electricity. And no-- hot or cold-- I mean, nothing like this. It was-- it was even the bathroom, the toilet, we had to go outside. It was an outhouse.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the-- the first pogrom , started was your grandfather involved in this at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My grandfather-- that's one thing I got to tell you. I remember like today, when the war started, it was in '39. And in '41, I think the-- when the Russians came in, my grandfather used to go every day-- he used to go to synagogue. And I had to make sure that I come in there every morning and every evening for the prayers. And I remember like, one time he came home. It was just a war. I mean, we didn't even expect it was going to be so bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: He came home from the synagogue on a Friday night. And he made Kiddish. You know what Kiddish means? You know the prayer, the Jewish prayer. And we started to-- this Shabbos party, the Sedar, what they call.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said grace. And then he said to us, while we were sitting, he said, my children, let me tell you-- in Jewish, he said. He said, my children, let me tell you. I know there's a very bad time coming on us. On you people, he says. And I hope I'm not going to be around then to see what is happening. And he passed out and he died right on the spot. That was Friday night. My grandma lived with us. She was about 86 years old. Then the Germans came in one time. They made a pogrom on a Saturday. Me and my father, we were already-- this is going on and this was in 19-- in the-- the end-- I think, in the middle of '41, or '40.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came in. And they took away mostly all the women and all the children. And then I was told that the Germans came in. And they wanted to take away my grandma. So my mother and sister, she say, you cannot take my mother without me. So they did her a favor. They took her with my grandmother with the two younger brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took them away to a ghetto. The ghetto was in Sulejow. That was about 8, 10 kilometers from our place. And then they killed them in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how they killed them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I heard. I mean, I never went there to look on it. But I know what-- the peasants were telling us they took them out in the forest. It was-- and [INAUDIBLE], it was called. That was outside of Sulejow. And they dug big trenches. And at that time, they killed quite a few thousand people. Because it was going on for a few days. They used to line them up by the edge of the trench with machine guns. And they gunned them down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you locate Sasow and the place where they took your mother in the ghetto, what larger town was this near in Poland or in the Ukraine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean what-- the-- what it's near, Sasow?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sasow is near-- well, you could-- there was a big town out of town. [INAUDIBLE], that was about 30 kilometers. Sulejow was about 10 kilometers. Lviv-- Lemberg-- it was about 50 kilometers from Sasow. As a matter of fact, our-- if you had to go-- if you want to go by train, you had to go through Sulejow. And from Sulejow, you could pick up to train to go to Lviv or any other places.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when this happened with your grandmother, mother and brothers--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you and your father stay in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We were-- we were already in-- in the camp, in the labor camp. That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me how that came about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- it was a labor camp. It was located in Sasow. And it was located in an area, it was called before, years ago-- which I don't even remember-- in Sasow was a big-- was famous for a big factory from paper. They used to make paper, pulp paper. And there was the man who the people, the family who owned the factory was called the [? Weiser ?] family. And they had a villa in the-- it was a nice place. It was outside the town. And there was a couple of big houses. And this is where the camp were. So they-- we were there. We used to go every morning. We used to go to the-- to cut the woods, the trees. Because we had a lot of forestry. There was a lot of forests nearby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to go in the morning. They used to-- they used to wake us up, five o'clock in the morning. By the way, we didn't even had SS in there. There was no SS or Gestapo. The only thing, what we had in there was one Ukrainian policeman. And the other one was made up of the Jewish police, you know? The camp [POLISH]. So we used to go every morning, 5:00 in the morning. We used to go out. Then at 10, 12 o'clock, we had lunch. Which if you didn't have the-- a few hundred people, by the time-- they only used to give you like one hour.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the time they came to you, you already was out of food. You didn't have-- so you had nothing to eat. So the food was, anyhow, it was nothing to brag about. There was even-- they used to give you a 1/4 pound of bread. The soup, they used to cook from the dead horses, or from any garbage, or whatever they had in there. And that was the food. But for some reason, I don't know, people were carrying on. And then we used to work on-- they destroyed the Jewish cemetery in Sasow. We used to crush the stones to make crushed stones for roads, to build for the Germans. That was going on in the camp. I was in the camp. And it started in '42 till '43, till July, '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My father was with me. And that's when they-- when they liquidated the camp. So you want me to tell you the story, how the camp was liquidated?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, that's a bad story. One morning they came down. They saw on that-- the Ukrainian with the Germans, they start onto the camp with machine guns, with tanks, like they were going in to a big battle. And they came down to us. And they woke everybody up. It started, already, like, maybe in the middle of the night. But we didn't know nothing, you know, until the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came down. And the one, the command-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- the-- the one who was in charge, his name was Obersturmfuhrer Vlasook.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] He is the one-- he was a guy. He was a little, short guy, but a big-- a very fat one. The worst dog, the worst enemy you could ever-- for human being. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: I have to just interrupt for a tape change.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
